Article.ArticleTitle: Cellulose based nanofiber-assisted stabilization of cationic ethosomes for skin penetration of bleomycin sulphate in the treatment of skin cancer.

Abstract.AbstractText: Skin cancer remains a significant global health issue, with its frequency steadily increasing in recent years. Nano-vesicular based drug delivery systems have significantly advanced the treatment of this life-threatening diseases. Despite their potential, the stability of these systems poses major challenge. Additionally, proper retention of formulation on skin is also a major concern. In our previous research, we developed cationic ethosomes loaded with bleomycin sulphate (BLM), a BCS Class III drug, aimed at treating skin cancer. This study focuses on enhancing the stability and skin retention of BLM-loaded cationic ethosomes by incorporating them into nanofibers. This approach aims to improve storage conditions and achieve sustain release action, thereby potentially increasing the efficacy of the treatment. Using thin film hydration, BLM-loaded cationic ethosomes were prepared and incorporated into nanofibers using electrospinning. Characterization involved surface morphology and drug release kinetics. Ex-vivo skin permeation and in-vitro studies in A431 cell lines were conducted, along with in-vivo anticancer activity assays. Results showed smooth surface morphology and enhanced stability of the nanofiber formulation. Ex-vivo assessments demonstrated favorable skin retention, while in-vitro studies showed improved cytotoxic effects. In-vivo studies on Swiss albino mice model and histopathological examination suggested suitability for topical use. These findings suggest that embedding cationic ethosomes in nanofibers not only stabilizes the formulation but also offers a good retention time of drug delivery system for skin cancer therapy.
